items.title: Bias
items.description: Increasing model complexity typically lowers bias — the hypothesis class can represent richer patterns. Bias owns the systematic-error term; complexity is the main dial that reduces it until diminishing returns.

items.title: Variance
items.description: Higher complexity on finite data usually raises variance — predictions swing more across training samples. Variance owns that instability term; complexity is the dial that often increases it past the sweet spot.

items.title: Decision trees
items.description: max_depth and leaf rules are direct complexity knobs — shallow trees underfit (high bias), deep trees overfit small tabular sets (high variance). See decision trees for split mechanics.

items.title: Polynomial regression
items.description: Polynomial degree is a classic complexity axis — degree 1 is low capacity, high degree is flexible curves. Validation curves over degree illustrate the U-shaped error pattern.

items.title: Underfitting vs overfitting
items.description: Too-low complexity causes underfitting (high train and val error); too-high complexity causes overfitting (low train, high val gap). This topic maps those regimes to capacity settings.

items.title: Cross-validation
items.description: K-fold CV estimates validation error across complexity settings more reliably than a single split — essential for picking depth, degree, or regularization strength.
